questionText: The reaction times, $X$ seconds, of drivers in a test are modelled by $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. It is found that $25\%$ of drivers react in under $0.20$ s and $80\%$ react in under $0.30$ s. (a) Find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to $3$ significant figures. (b) Find the probability that a randomly chosen driver takes more than $0.28$ s to react.

steps.description: State the model and the two given facts
steps.explanation: Reaction times are normal with unknown $\mu$ and $\sigma$. ‚Äò$25\%$ under $0.20$ s‚Äô gives $P(X<0.20)=0.25$ and ‚Äò$80\%$ under $0.30$ s‚Äô gives $P(X<0.30)=0.80$. Both are lower-tail proportions.
Standardising $0.20$ with $Z=\dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ preserves the lower-tail area of $0.25$.
The lower quartile of the standard normal is negative because a quarter of the area lies below it, to the left of the mean.
Equating the standardised value of $0.20$ with $z_1$ gives the first equation.

steps.description: Standardise the second condition and read z
steps.explanation: The second statement is already lower-tail, so we standardise $0.30$ and read $z_2$ directly. As $80\%>50\%$ the value is positive.
Equating the standardised value of $0.30$ with $z_2$ gives the second simultaneous equation.
Subtracting the two ‚Äòvalue $-\ \mu = z\sigma$‚Äô forms removes $\mu$; the $0.10$ s gap spans about $1.52$ standard deviations.
Dividing the time gap by the standardised gap gives a standard deviation of about $0.0660$ s.
From the first equation $\mu = 0.20 + 0.6745\,\sigma$. The mean reaction time of about $0.244$ s lies above $0.20$, consistent with a quarter of drivers being faster than $0.20$ s.
To three significant figures the model is $X \sim N(0.244,\ 0.0660^2)$ seconds.

steps.description: Set up part (b) and standardise 0.28 s
steps.explanation: For part (b) we need an upper-tail probability, so we standardise $0.28$ using the parameters just found.
Since $0.28$ is a little above the mean of $0.244$, its standardised value is modestly positive.
The area to the right of a point just above the mean is a little under a half; here it is about $0.293$.
So roughly $29.3\%$ of drivers take longer than $0.28$ s to react, which is plausible for a time a little above the mean.
